content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies where some people are enclosed in a small area?

**Score:** 40
**Body:** Edit:

Oh my goodness! I wasn't expecting so many replies. Thank you all so much! I think I'm good with suggestions, seems like people are repeating a lot of them. Please no more recommendations! Unless you think you have a rare one. 

Thank you all again. There's just no way that I can reply to all of you, so thank you all!

Something like 12 Angry Men, or Rope is more what I'm looking for.

Not looking for something like Clue, the characters have the whole mansion to potentially walk around. Or Coherence, where they can walk outside.

Reservoir Dogs is technically in one area, once the group arrives, but the story is told through flashbacks. So while it's a great movie, not exactly what I'm looking for.

More looking for people to be in a vehicle, like a bus, train, etc. Or in an elevator or something. Maybe even in a bunker for most of the movie, would be okay. An Apartment or house is fine, as long as they stay in pretty much one area.

It can be animated or live action. I don't care what the decade is. If it's in English or in another language.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Cube (1997)?
2. *12 Angry Men*
3. Panic room 
4. Room
5. Das Boot
6. Speed
7. Buried
8. Devil
9. 10 Cloverfield lane
10. Hateful 8!!

## 2. Suggest me a life-changing movie, no matter how long it is.

**Score:** 37
**Body:** I want movies to meditate about life and situations, movies like ''Lost in translation'', ''Her'', and ''Florida Project'' would be great for me. Thanks! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Good Will Hunting (1997)
2. Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind 
3. About Time. 
4. City of god
5. Shawshank redemption 
6. Cinema Paradiso 

Once Upon a Time in America

Spirited Away

Boogie Nights 
7. Embrace of the serpent

Night of the hunter
8. Koyaanisqatsi 
9. Boyhood
10. Man on Wire

I used to watch this to go to sleep at night. Philippe Petit is a legend 

## 3. I am a huge fan of the "One Man vs. The System" movies. What gems have I missed?

**Score:** 33
**Body:** \-Cool Hand Luke 

\-One Flew Over The Cuckoo's Nest 

\-The Shawshank Redemption

\-First Blood

\-On The Waterfront

\-Next of Kin

\-Dark City

\-Falling Down

\-The Last Castle

\-My Cousin Vinnie

\-Eastern Promises

\-The Crow (no...not THAT one)

\-John Wick 1 & 2

Any suggestions to add to my list are greatly appreciated.  Thanks! 😎

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. -	The Count of Monte Cristo (2002) the 2024 series is amazing too
-	The Running Man (1987 & 2025)
-	The Truman Show
-	Snowpiercer
-	They Live
2. 'V for Vendetta' (2005)
3. How is nobody mentioning Minority Report???
4. Network (1976)  is a great film on these themes. 
  - I’m mad as hell and I’m not going to take it anymore!
  - That “the world is a business” monologue is very memorable & continues to be relevant
5. The Negotiator, underrated Samuel L. Jackson movie.

John Q
6. They Live!
7. Hell or High Water - 2016

Not exactly one man vs. but still the same vibe. 
8. Enemy of the State

## 4. If you love claymation movies, then you need to see the best and most underrated one: Mary and Max

**Score:** 27
**Body:** Mary and Max… man.

Saying too much can ruin it, all i’m going to say is that it’s a great and emotional film that’s made in traditional claymation, and has a lot to say about neurodivergence, trauma and loneliness while still managing to be incredibly uplifting. Probably the funniest movie i’ve ever watched too.

It’s my favourite film of all time for a reason and i beg everyone, no matter who you are, to put aside time to watch this criminally underrated masterpiece.

It’s so incredibly comforting and warm, so make sure to get a warm blanket and some hot coco too

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Just googled it and already love the vibe. Also the cast of voice actors are stacked i can't believe this is the first time I heard about this film, thanks for recommending
2. It's puppets, but you may enjoy *Anomalisa* (2015) as well.
3. There's also Memoir of a Snail which is from the same director as Mary Max (Adam Elliot)
4. What a beautiful and emotional movie! I’ve watched around ten years ago. Absolutely recommended. 
5. Mary and Max is so good. 

Memoir of a Snail is good, as well. It doesn't quite hit in the same as Mary and Max, but it's not too far off.
6. Agreed. Funny, thoughtful, quirky, and genuinely moving.  It's criminally underrated.
7. Wow I’ve never heard of this and it looks lovely. I’ll watch it soon! Thank you for the recommendation 
8. Such a beautiful film based on a heartwrenching true story. It wrecked me.
9. Be sure to grab a can of sweetened condensed milk to enjoy with the show. 
10. Mary & Max is such a beautiful film. I watch it about once a year and laugh and cry every single time.

## 5. Need a Mind-Blowing Thriller Movie Suggestion 🎬🖤

**Score:** 29
**Body:** Looking for a thriller movie that will keep me on the edge of my seat and mess with my mind till the end 😵‍💫🔥

Suggest me your best thriller movie… something unforgettable! 👀

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Fincher’s “The Game”
  - [removed]
    - It’s a ridiculous movie, but extremely entertaining 
2. Shutter Island
3. Primal Fear
4. I Saw The Devil
5. Insomnia

Wind River

The Frozen Ground 

One Hour Photo 
6. If you haven't seen it, Seven. 
7. Andhadhun
8. The korean movie Oldboy from 2003

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What actor/actress got cast in a role that everyone believed they were miscast or not qualified to take on, but knocked it out of the park and everyone IMMEDIATELY changed their minds after seeing their performance?

**Score:** 1148
**Body:** I’m not talking about hindsight where 5, 10, 20 years later we’ve come around and regard their performance as legendary. I’m talking walking into the theater with legitimate ire and walking out in awe and utterly impressed. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Bruce Willis - Die Hard

![gif](giphy|l3vR0SIMQhrBwu1hK)
  - This was a legendarily panned casting choice at the time. Good example. 
    - Didn’t the studio threaten to shut down the movie after his casting or something? I know one heard his casting caused a shit storm, I just don’t know how big it was internally
2. 99% of comic book movie castings 
  - Chris Evans as Captain America when he originally played Johnny Storm was hilarious on the first watch.
3. Who thought Ellen Ripley was miscast? Sigourney was basically an unknown when Alien came out and no one expected her character to be the last woman standing… 
4. Anne Rice publicly complained when Tom Cruise was cast as Lestat.  When the movie came out she then apologized.  He was great.
5. Heath Ledger- Joker
6. Michael Keaton-Batman
7. Bob odenkirk. Who knew he could be an action star

## 2. What are your thoughts on this film, Watchmen?  It’s a solid adaptation, and honestly, it might be Zack Snyder’s best film.

**Score:** 769
**Body:** what do you think?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|PwhzKJ3N6cLFS)

Jackie Earle Haley steals the movie
  - If Jack Earle Healy stole anything Rorschach would drop him off a building.
  - He was unbelievable in that role, his character was fantastic. Dark, complex, unforgiving, brutal, but had an unwavering moral compass. Arguably my favorite fictional character next to the Joker.
  - One of the most under utilised talent in industry! 
2. You’re locked in here with me!
  - I will never forget the reaction to that line in my theater when I saw this on opening night . Crowd went wild 
3. Ultimate Cut is easily a top 10 comic book movie. It is undeniably Zack's best movie. I'm glad that people are coming around and reevaluating it. 
4. I enjoyed it.    The opening credits especially.
5. Dude hangs dong.
6. I think its hilarious that Nite Owl's sex scene, that in the comics shows his erotic fixation on macho superheroing, needing a costume to cum, is depicted as a "hey this is a cool thing"

## 3. Luke Evans is so underrated. Often cast in supporting roles, but still never fails to deliver.

**Score:** 406
**Body:** From Fast and Furious 6-8, Beauty and the Beast, and The Hobbit, among others, he may either play antagonistic or supporting roles most of the time, but he still portrays these roles very well. He has the manly, rugged looks and oozing sex appeal of a leading man but has the range and versatility of a character actor.

What are your thoughts on him so far and what's your favorite role of his?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dracula Untold is amazing but it seems like no one watched it. 
  - I, personally, enjoyed the movie very much. Out of all the Dracula themed movies, this, FINALLY, was something new, fresh. 
  - If Dracula Untold had come out in the early 2000s during that vampire phase I think it would have done very well and we would have had a trilogy.

Either that or if a different director were at the helm.

I think the movie starts off pretty strong, but it falls apart so much so quickly.
2. He should play Doctor Doom.
3. Him playing Gaston was the best part of the live action Beauty and the Beast. 
4. He’s about to play Dr. Frank N Furter in Rocky Horror on broadway 🎭

https://preview.redd.it/24l60dhlokvg1.jpeg?width=1586&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=dce61f89b2c81c03f7767ea463b8df117c1ba6e8
5. He could have been a great Bond. 
  - Yes, he could have also made a great Bond villain.
6. He is great in the Apple TV mini series Echo 3
7. Ya why is he not bigger?

## 4. After watching "Wuthering Heights," I wondered if Jacob Elordi can actually act or if he's just a sex symbol, and the first three episodes of Euphoria have only reinforced that idea. What do you think?

**Score:** 221

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It was nice to see him play someone who wasn’t a horrible human being in Frankenstein (2025). 

  - He was awesome as the Monster in Frankenstein
  - I never cared about him until Frankenstein. He was sublime in it. I cannot stand him in any other piece of media. 

He needs to take a note out of the Skarsgaard Son playbook, lean into the height and pretty face and just play monsters. 

Alex was a vampire, Bill is Pennywise. 

All three of them would make AWESOME space marines in Henry Cavill’s 40k project. 
2. I think he's a good actor, he was great in Frankenstein. I just think he gets miscast as the "hot badboy". While he's definitely a good looking guy, I don't think he has the charisma or "aura" to pull off those roles. He worked way, way better as the vulnerable monster in Frankenstein.
3. He actually was excellent in “The Narrow Road to the Deep North.” So much so that I didn’t even know it was him until I looked it up. 
4. He was pretty good in Priscilla and Frankenstein
  - I would watch the hell out of Priscilla and Frankenstein. 
5. In Frankenstein he was very good. I also think he delivered in Saltburn. For the rest… let’s see. 
  - WOW I forgot he was in Saltburn. Yeah, agree he was good in both of those. Seems kinda eh in Euphoria 
6. He was terrible in Wuthering Heights, but equally very miscast. Good in Saltburn

## 5. Can we talk about how great the practical effects are in The Thing (1982)?

**Score:** 222
**Body:** I’ve just watched The Thing for the first time. Absolutely amazing. Especially the production design, the storyline obviously and the practical effects. I also noticed the color contrast between red and blue in the movie which I really liked. 

What do you think of the practical effects in The Thing (1982)? 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Frequently discussed as the best practical effects ever on Hollywood. 
  - Yeah exactly, lol. Can we talk about it? We haven't *stopped* talking about it. 
    - Went to the universal make up show in universal recently and they gave them a nod too! 

GOAT
    - No shit. The thing is probably the most talked about movie on Reddit. 
2. https://preview.redd.it/g8y0r14b0mvg1.jpeg?width=960&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=198afb8dddd897bf8813ea2110094a69d3c62dce

Here's a still of the stop motion Blair Thing. It was mostly cut out of the movie because I guess it looked a bit too much like a stop motion effect.
3. Beautiful. As a fan of SFX, they really put in the work. This movie is worth watching for the practical effects alone. Nice bonus that it is also just a great movie. 
4. this movie will always have the best sfx. there is no going back with modern tech - they just aren't invested in this type of stuff anymore it's a real shame. 

20/10 best sfx, best movie
5. 👍👍
6. They were amazing! My favorite was the spider/head thingy. 
7. I told everyone back in 82, good to see I was right all these years later….

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. One of the biggest pleasures in contemporary film was realizing part way through that Fincher's The Killer (2023) was a satire

**Score:** 274
**Body:** Once we realized this the film became not only so much more enjoyable - poking subtle fun at many of those who came to it hoping for a slick action/assassin film and were disappointed - it became hilarious. Fassbinder's character is constantly, arrogantly wrong and Internet meme trite in his self-talk. In the first few viewings though - its so good I've watched it many times - t I just thought it was just satirizing Melville's Le Samouraï (1967) but last night I watched The American (2010) and saw so many other touch points like the "in a great shape for a man his age" self-workout scenes (though there is certainly a nod to Brad Pitt's shirt-off moment from Fight Club too), the tourism travelog feels, and the ways The American was probably reflecting the precision-aura of Le Samouraï too. To further flesh it out the humor, as I've commented elsewhere a few months ago:

*When he says that he has to wait until his heartrate beats per minute to get down to 60 (? I forget the number), but then he fires the rifle at something like 68 and misses. Listening to the Smiths Meat is Murder while attempting to assassinate someone. The way he acts like an expert on everything, just like internet commenters (but totally guessing the dog's weight and dose wrong). The rich guy taking a limo to the gym, which is a 5 minute walk away (shown by the phone). The abandoned (Unicorn Finance) WeWork office. The killer eating "keto" (just eating the burger patty from fastfood, if I recall)...The killer is a successful gig-economy worker who lives the fantasy beach house dream. But he's totally lame. Does his yoga, measures his beats-per-minute on his watch, quotes his adages, eats his keto, guesses wrong at everything. He's a very, very subtle (not over the top) Inspector Clouseau. He's a bourgeois killer, like if that ultra, ultra flexible guy in your yoga class was also an international assassin.*

In any case, the satire key really unlocks this film, a correction perhaps of how many who loved Fight Club but did not realize that it was satire about Toxic Masculinity, the very thing they loved it for. And watching George Clooney's The American made it even better. It actually is posing through satire a strong critique of the hyper-Capitalism consumer "subject", opening up the notion that we are all self-satisfied, non-reflective "killers" in our lifestyle.

A recommendation: if you enjoyed The Killer and haven't seen The American, do (its a pretty good slow burn, beautiful film in its own right).

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Omg this post may encourage a rewatch for me at some point. I remember seeing the killer when it came out and finding it verryyy odddd how the film treats its main subject as a cool professional when his actions display the exact opposite. I just thought it was written poorly…
  - How did people not get this was a satire on the hitman genre when watching it. 
    - While watching it I didn't necessarily think "this is totally satire", instead I thought it was more of a dark/absurd comedy. Looking back on it now with all of these details pointed out I can see and appreciate the satire, but I think the movie is just subtle enough that the satire can be missed.
  - Yeah I noticed protagonist not being super accurate at his shit and how over the top his Smiths obsession gets to absurdity. 
But didn’t watch through the satire lens. Was prolly stoned. 
But a rewatch is order now. thanks for the post OP

2. Yep, I really like it even though it’s not top tier Fincher, and it’s the way it subverts everything that makes it so fun. The nailgun scene is hysterical for that reason… a guy who thinks he’s some uber-in-charge assassin who actually doesn’t really know what he’s doing.
3. You could also look at the movie and I know this is tired, but you could look at it as a metaphor for filmmaking. 

He’s a consummate professional that absolutely has his methods, routine, and style nailed down and even though he’s the best he does still make mistakes and you could even excuse it because things weren’t exactly perfect to start with. But he is also an expert in improvising and planning out contingencies. “It rained on the day so what is the plan for getting this scene inside?” 

His keto adjacent diet also reflects the commonly held practice among experienced crew members to not eat carbs while working because it makes you more tired than it helps and makes you gain weight. (A lot of the job is waiting to get the shot and moving on to the next location very quickly.) 

My favorite expression of this metaphor is how his boss takes two people with completely different styles and just thinks that putting them together at huge expense means it will get the job done. Like calling in whoever’s available and good enough when you need to fix something in reshoots. 
  - To further this idea slightly, I believe the killer missing is a filmmaker putting out a flop or dud, and trying to course correct to prove he can still do his job and isn’t getting rusty. In that way I think it can connect to Fincher directly with Mank being disregarded comparatively to his filmography, showing that he can still get the job done and release a quality product in The Killer.
4. The ultimateconfirmation that it IS indeed a satire is him saying this as the first line of the film: “If you are not able to endure boredom then this work is not for you”

It then immediately jump cuts to him falling asleep lmao
5. Same! A little bit like American Psycho where the self talk doesnt overlay with the reality.

You realize he is eating mcdonald's and sneaking into equinox, and shooting from a wework. And then he holds the bottom socio economic guys to the strictest punishment and lets the guy at the top off with a warning.
  - >And then he holds the bottom socio economic guys to the strictest punishment and lets the guy at the top off with a warning.

Wow, I didn't actually pick up on that. That is a very good note. 

## 2. Question about an editing/camera technique in “The Night of the Hunter” (1955)

**Score:** 21
**Body:** Just watched this for the first time last night and I was truly enamored by it. The fairytale quality to it, the dichotomy between Ms. Cooper and Powell, John as a character, and his representation of childhood innocence, vulnerability, and resilience.

One thing that really struck me was the visual style of the film. It seemed between being reverential to past eras ie the silent film era, and being so progressive and innovative stylistically, that it’s hard for me to compare it to contemporary American films of the era.

One very simple shot which really has left me in wonder is a simple fade transition, starting with a pan up to a starlit cloudless night into a splendid sunrise from behind clouds. The type of shot that has been done many times before and after this film. What gets me is, that the sky in these two shots, appear as if they are the \*exact\* same sky shot with the same framing and angle and everything. As if they got the night shot, and left the camera there overnight without changing the settings and shot the daytime shot.

Like I said, I’ve seen shots like this so many times, yet I’ve never been struck by such a sense of continuity like that. How could they achieve this effect, without a reference object through which I could compare perspective?

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Great film. Hard to know without a screenshot but if it’s the shot I’m thinking of, it’s done on a sound stage. 

The one after the kids flee? They have gone down the river and are waking up? 

I may be wrong, there’s a lot of trick photography in the film, highly recommend watching the UCLA documentary by Robert Gitt. 

It’s actually of the best docs I’ve ever seen because it’s done chronologically, in-line with the film. I’ll be back in a couple of minutes with some links.

Edit, ok - Couple of examples from the doc:

[how they did the underwater shot](https://www.reddit.com/u/ydkjordan/s/jyxzaBIDKi)

[outtake of Shelley really bringing it](https://www.reddit.com/u/ydkjordan/s/6J0fmM6XPS)

[Laughton talking to Shelley](https://www.reddit.com/r/CineShots/s/wENDDgrZXd)

Honestly, watch anything Gitt has done on classic films, cheers and good luck!
  - Yes! That is the shot I believe. After they escape from the barnhouse after the iconic shot of Powell’s silhouette on horseback singing “Leanin on the Everlasting Arms”. And John says something like “Don’t they ever sleep?” before John and Pearl fall asleep on the boat. Another symbol for children being at the whims of a world run by adults.

That’s a lot of words to say, “Yes that’s right” but I’m really just so amazed at the beauty of this film. The story crescendos so perfectly that it has this Shakespearian quality, as if it existed for all time and was just waiting for an artist to chisel it into existence.
    - That shot of Powell's silhouette on horseback was done with a small pony and short adult stand in to shift the scale to appear farther away on the soundstage.
      - Amazing!
  - The underwater shot is also exquisitely beautiful. Evocative of the ending of L’Atalante, it led me to believe it was the ending of the film. Only for the film to, from there, enter it’s most beautiful passage in the third act. I will gladly check out all of these suggestions, thank you for sharing <3 
  - Thanks for sharing!

## 3. Dunkirk (2017) - Review

**Score:** 9
**Body:** So, 4th watching of Dunkirk - And it holds up pretty well every time.

We have seen a lot of great war movies treat wars in different ways. Some are patriotic, some are humanist, some are anti-war movies and so on. Dunkirk is about survival.

We are dropped directly into the streets of Dunkirk. Some young soldiers are trying to find some water to drink. They are surrounded by the  enemy. It needs not be named as it simply does not matter. What matters is that these young blokes - who are barely soldiers and were thrown into the war through mass recruitment - are staring directly into the eyes of death. And boom, bullets are flying out of nowhere. And that's the nature of the whole movie. Bullets, torpedos and bombs are coming out of nowhere. You are not fighting. You are just surviving. And if you survive those, you might drown in the ocean. And Nolan makes s tremendous job of putting you in the shoes of those soldiers and you feel the horror that one might go through in those moments.

There is no glory in war here. You see boys who are trying everything to survive - adopting identities of dead soldiers, carrying wounded soldiers just so they can get the chance to get on the ship, sneaking into the ships and so on. There is a scene where Harry Styles and his regimental brothers ask Gibson to get out of the boat, claiming Tommy would be next if needed. You wonder whether they will choose someone among themselves next if needed. 

That's just how the war pushes you into a corner. We see Cilian Murphy going berserk in that civilian boat. He shouts and panicks and throws hands resulting in George's death. A couple of shots later (although few days earlier in timeline), we see the same character calmly asking Alex and Tommy and others to be patient when their ship gets hit by torpedo. But he was not the same man in Mr Dawson's boat after encountering death so closely. 

Dunkirk is not all grim. You see people trying to help others even when pushed to their limits. Gibson rushes to open the door of the cabin below the deck after their ship is hit by a torpedo. Alex after threatening to kill Gibson, signals him to get out of the sinking boat, though it proved too late in the end. Farrier sacrifices himself to save the soldiers. And thousands of civilians risk their lives to save their fellow countrymen. 

Dunkirk features some magnificent sequences - the opening shot, the bombing on the Dunkirk beach shot and the breathtaking dogfights of fighter jets. Dialogue writing has been one of the weakest aspects of Nolan's film making (imo, poor dialogues hold back Interstellar from being a masterpiece). Here, dialogues are minimal but impactful. Hans Zimmer doesn't let your ears rest though with his constant edge of the seat thrilling score. The story cross-cuts among three different timelines in classic Nolan fashion - though it adds to the chaos of war.

When they are eventually saved, soldiers are disappointed as they failed to serve their country properly. Alex even projects this disappointment on the blind old bloke claiming he did not even look at him due to embarrassment. But sometimes, survival is enough. After all, they were not well trained soldiers but rather young boys. 

And the movie ends with some really beautiful shots with  Tommy reading Churchill's famous speech (F\*\*\* Churchill though). One of the greatest war movies ever made.

"Is he a coward, Mr Dawson?"

"He's shell-shocked, George. He's not himself. He may never be himself again."

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. the ticking clock throughout the entire movie is what does it for me. Zimmer went absolutely insane with that score, it basically functions as another character. the way the tension just never lets up from the first scene to the last is something most war films dont even attempt
  - zimmer uses the tick to crazy effect. the scene in the thin red line where the americans attack a japanese camp through fog while the score tick tocks is chill inducing to me
2. Perhaps the greatest tragedy of all, is that the so called "Great War" was so horrific, so traumatizing, that it's survivors were sure that nothing like it would never happen again, only for the peace to be lost in less than a generation.  A father at Dunkirk, or Verdunne, and a son at Normandy, or Midway, etc.

Generals who grew up on tales of cavalry charges, breaking the enemy lines with nothing but saber and willpower, now sending men against machine gun bunkers and chemical weapons.

And here we are, over a century later, and nothing has changed.  It's maddening. 
3. Agreed with pretty much everything you say here. It’s definitely Nolan’s best film, and mainly for that reason you mention: minimal dialogue and so minimal chance for Nolan to embarrass himself with a corny line akin to Ann Hathaway’s greetings-card wisdom on the power of love in Interstellar, the relentless explanation of the premise in Inception and that time in Tenet where a character says “<unintelligible>”

And yet, he still manages to take a torpedo to an otherwise solid film with a single word delivered by Kenneth Branagh: “home”. It’s not just a cheesy moment, it’s a horribly jingoistic one that undermines the film’s realism with a reminder that the film is a representation of the mythology of Dunkirk rather than Dunkirk itself. 

The film is a wonderful spectacle but that single line made me question the entire project. Do we really, after all, need another retelling of this story? Is this what Britain needs? 
  - Beach is empty, there's supposed to be hundreds of thousands there and tons of equipment. It really lowers the impact imo, it's a bigger problem than a cheesy line.
    - Do you realise that those hundreds of thousands of people were spread along 15 km of coastline? This is such a cinema sin nitpick 

## 4. Wes Anderson's Literary References?

**Score:** 6
**Body:** I'm curious about where Wes Anderson is pulling his literary references from, it feels like there's a ton of books that inspire the kinds of stories he tells in his filmmaking in less than obvious ways and I was wondering if other folks have clocked any.

There's some I know about, JD Salinger's stories about the Glass family seem like a big reference point for the Royal Tenenbaums and the writer Stefan Zweig seems to be the touchstone for the writer in the Grand Budapest Hotel. Can anyone think of some others?

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Life Aquatic's gotta be based on Moby Dick, right? 

There's a great thread on the Wes sub about his literary nods 

https://www.reddit.com/r/wesanderson/comments/15lhoea/master_list_of_wes_anderson_literary/

I like the suggestion that the museum scene in Tennenbaums was based on Mixed-Up Files

Also these writers are listed in French Dispatch:

James Baldwin

Harold Ross

William Shawn

Rosamond Bernier

Mavis Gallant

A.J. Liebling

S.N. Behrman

Lillian Ross

Janet Flanner

Luc Sante

James Thurber

Joseph Mitchell

Wolcott Gibbs

St. Clair McKelway

Ved Mehta

Brendan Gill

E.B. White

Katharine White
  - Of course Wes Anderson likes Thurber. I don't know why I didn't think of that before.
2. Reading a description of Thornton Wilder’s Our Town it seems that there’s little doubt that that’s an influence on Wes Anderson.

It may be mostly that there are some visual similarities rather than tonal, but Picnic by William Inge, film directed by Joshua Logan, seems like an influence on Asteroid City. Inge and Logan and Marylin Monroe’s Bus Stop, and the Monroe film The Misfits might be references too.

3. The French Dispatch is pretty clearly a love letter to the golden age of literary journalism and the culture around the New Yorker and the Paris Review. 

The Life Aquatic owes a lot to those illustrated picture books of the 70s and 80s with intricately drawn cutaways of things like castle life and ocean exploring ships. (Can’t recall the name of the specific series of books or if it was a general trend across multiple series? But I loved em..)
4. He has actually published books for each of his last few movies with exactly this sort of stuff in it. Collections of essays, short stories, etc. I have a few of them and they make for great reading. 
  - Oh, really? I had no idea, could I ask what they're called? Are they like those art books or something else?
    - No, these are collections of period essays and short stories that were inspo material for the projects. One is called Do Not Detonate, the other that I have is called An Editor’s Burial.

## 5. AI screenwriting and the case for more improv filmmaking

**Score:** 4
**Body:** I've been thinking about what AI screenwriting actually means for film. It's everywhere now, screenplays, books, emails, and it's almost expected. But if the written script becomes a cheap, fast, disposable part of the process, what becomes valuable is everything the script was never able to capture anyway, what Terrence Malick was always chasing, the ephemeral moments.

I made a feature film last year almost entirely through improvisation. The cast had situations, not dialogue. My starting point was Cassavetes, specifically the idea that if you give an actor the exact words, you get exactly those words back and nothing more. Admittedly I did write a few scenes, and one of them is probably my favourite in the whole film, but if you put someone in a situation and apply real pressure, asking for genuine reactions, you get something you can't plan for. You catch people instead of posing them. Which is why Malick and Cassavetes films do what they do.

What that process taught me is that the most interesting thing on a film set can be the thing that wasn't written. I remember waiting for the natural moment to call cut and everyone being so excited, like that was incredible, or bursting out laughing after a take that had been completely serious. Sometimes it's a choice an actor makes, or a silence that lands harder than any line, or the moment where something real just breaks through.

I hope that as studios race to make the first AI written script a box office hit, more filmmakers respond by abandoning the script altogether. Not because it is trendy but because the constraint forces you toward something more honest. Cassavetes understood this in 1968 and if you've read Accidental Genius you'll know he was not blasé about it. His films can be tough to get through but there is something genuinely captivating in them that I haven't found anywhere else.

Curious whether others see the same opening here. I don't know if any other filmmakers on here have had that same liberating experience, but if you have I'd genuinely like to hear about it.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Have you seen the work of Joel Haver? He is a filmmaker working primarily on youtube and much of his stuff is unscripted or minimally scripted. I really love his work and his spirit, and I see him as a key part of an essentially new movement of internet filmmaking. He also has a deep love for Casavettes. 

David Lynch’s “Inland Empire” also comes to mind. Shot with no script and mostly just David shooting a camera at Laura Dern, it evolves into this just monumental feat of storytelling with so many twisting narratives and nuanced characters. There is a beautiful emergent quality that happens when you just shoot things with a camera, and then coalesce them in the editing room.
  - Not to long ago Nolan wanted to do that for Dunkirk but considering the scope of the production they decided differently with his wife. 

What I don’t understand from OP's post is… what does AI screenwriting mean in this case? That as it becomes more prominent it can inspire filmmakers to focus on trying to do cinema as much as possible in real time with their cameras to try to capture the ephemeral moments of reality? Rather than following a script specially those generated by AI. 
  - With Joel Haver the Cassavetes love is visible in everything he does and I think you're right that he represents something genuinely new and the YouTube distribution model suits his work in a way that traditional releases never would.

What did you make of the Cassavetes direct influence on Haver's stuff specifically? Curious which films you'd point to.
2. This approach would not work for every type of film. It's certainly a technique that should be used and explored but it will never be ubiquitous. Not all actors are able to improvise even with a script so I would expect the results to be hit or miss. I can also imagine the pain this will be for the Editor.

I would like to correct you on the common misinterpretation of Cassavetes and his approach. Shadows is the closest he ever got to full improvisation, but even that film had a script and rehearsal. I would have to search for it but I've both read and seen him specifically state that he rehearses with the actors from a script.

Malick as well works with a script. The difference is that a lot of his scenes don't require dialogue and his technique for cinematography allows for more spontaneous work from the actors.

Don't view this as a criticism of Cassavetes or Malick as they are two of my favorite Directors. I think their approach is different and we can see those differences in their work. I don't see AI script generation pushing filmmakers to improvisation since that approach takes a lot of patience and a willingness to craft a film with the knowledge that the end result will be vastly different than perhaps what was envisioned. 
  - You're right and I appreciate the correction. I oversimplified the Cassavetes approach and I knew it when I was writing. Shadows is the closest he got to full improvisation and even that had structure underneath it. The more accurate thing to say is that he built a process around rehearsal and situation that gave actors genuine latitude within a framework, which is different from no script at all. Malick is the same, the spontaneity comes from his approach to shooting and what he asks of actors within scenes, not from an absence of written material.

What I was really describing, and probably should have been more precise about, is a spectrum. Most of the filmmakers I admire work somewhere between a fixed script and genuine freedom, and the interesting question is where on that spectrum they sit and why.

On the editing point, you are completely right. It is a significant amount of pain. The ratio of shot material to used material is brutal and the editor carries most of that weight. On my film the edit was where the structure finally emerged, which was exciting but also took a long time and a lot of patience.

I think improvisation suits some stories and some actors and not others. I suppose what I was really asking is whether the AI moment creates any cultural permission for the filmmakers who are drawn to that approach to pursue it more openly. Maybe that's a smaller shift than I was suggesting.
3. Yeah that would be a phenomenal outcome. Film history is filled with filmmakers who actually sought to use the art form to convey emotion, not just to create a facsimile of a script. In so many run-of-the-mill Hollywood productions one may as well just read the script, the “film” is merely a visualization of it in a very bland and cookie-cutter manner, not an exploration of the art form of film.

This is a great hope.
